title: Anchor-Price Sequencing

content.statement: The first number a customer sees shapes all future price perception.
content.question: What anchor price or reference do your business members see BEFORE the membership price?
content.equation: APS = (Anchor Strength × Contrast × Deal Value) ÷ Rejection
sop: Map every price-related number a prospect sees before your actual price (competitor prices, regular price, premium tier, market rate).
Rate the Anchor Strength of that first number 1-10, how memorable and credible it is (see calculation, page 6).
Rate the Contrast 1-10, how different your price looks next to the anchor (see calculation, page 6).
Rate the Deal Value 1-10, how good your offer feels once the anchor is in place (see calculation, page 6).

Rate Rejection risk 1-10, how likely the anchor itself is to scare people off before they reach your price (see calculation, page 6), then redesign the sequence if Rejection outweighs the other three.
content.explainer: Never present your price first. Set the context, then your price looks cheap by comparison.
questions: What anchor price or reference do your business members see BEFORE the membership price?
If a customer never saw a higher-priced option first, would your actual price still feel like a good deal?
What's the very first number, price, comparison, or example, a prospect sees before they see yours?

definitions.def: The first number a person sees shapes how they evaluate later numbers.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Anchor Strength 8, Contrast 7, Deal Value 6. 8 times 7 times 6 equals 336, divided by Rejection 3 equals 112. Higher scores mean your price sequence makes your actual offer feel like a clear win. Lower scores mean the anchor is either too weak to matter or strong enough to scare people away before they see your price.
content.closing_statement: Whatever number they see first becomes their yardstick.

caption:
Principle Nº 62: Anchor-Price Sequencing

The first number a customer sees shapes all future price perception. Never present your price first.

Ask yourself: What anchor price or reference do your business members see BEFORE the membership price?
